title: Mastery Exam: Pseudo-Classes and Pseudo-Elements
summary: Mastery Exam: Pseudo-Classes and Pseudo-Elements retrieves prior skills in an original independent selector and state release decision scenario and requires implementation, behavior, failure-state, and transfer evidence.
objectives: Apply css action pseudo classes, css structural pseudo classes, css form state pseudo classes, css functional pseudo classes, css pseudo elements in unfamiliar constraints.
Produce or audit a real independent selector and state release decision artifact without happy-path-only reasoning.
Preserve earlier requirements while testing keyboard, narrow-width, invalid, and assistive-semantic states.

starterFiles.html: <!-- Continue the Community Needs Survey state work here. -->
starterFiles.css: /* Continue the survey state and selector system here. */

steps.title: 01 · debug 1: survey action-state and focus-route audit · quiz
steps.instruction: Checkpoint 1: Which claim survives the changed case? Scenario: independent selector and state release decision.
steps.why: Mastery Exam: Pseudo-Classes and Pseudo-Elements makes the decision observable and testable. This is checkpoint 1 in independent selector and state release decision.

content.title: independent selector and state release decision evidence
content.text: For Mastery Exam: Pseudo-Classes and Pseudo-Elements, choose the css action pseudo classes claim that preserves the independent selector and state release decision purpose, native behavior, and understandable relationships when presentation changes.

hints: Mastery Exam: Pseudo-Classes and Pseudo-Elements checkpoint 1: identify the user task and data relationship first.
Mastery Exam: Pseudo-Classes and Pseudo-Elements checkpoint 1: compare source, native behavior, and announced meaning.
Mastery Exam: Pseudo-Classes and Pseudo-Elements checkpoint 1: make the smallest repair that preserves earlier passing work.

options.text: Independent selector and state release decision case 1: hover and focus represent the same interaction for every user.

Independent selector and state release decision case 1: define each state from its actual input or navigation condition, keep focus visible, and test pointer, touch, keyboard, and fragment navigation separately.
Independent selector and state release decision case 1: put every interaction style under hover because browsers synthesize hover on all devices.

options.text: independent selector and state release decision phase 2.1: list the target element siblings in DOM order
independent selector and state release decision phase 2.2: identify whether the selector counts all children or one element type
independent selector and state release decision phase 2.3: apply the formula and selector filtering in the correct order
independent selector and state release decision phase 2.4: insert remove and hide nodes and verify the resulting matches

options.text: Independent selector and state release decision case 3: invalid styling should appear before a learner has interacted with a blank form.
Independent selector and state release decision case 3: distinguish requirement, interaction, validity, selection, and availability states, then time feedback and pair it with explicit text.
Independent selector and state release decision case 3: hide disabled controls completely so users never wonder why they are unavailable.

options.text: Independent selector and state release decision case 4: the where function adds the specificity of its most specific argument.
Independent selector and state release decision case 4: choose is, where, not, or has from matching intent, calculate resulting specificity, and test both intended and excluded component states.
Independent selector and state release decision case 4: put an ID inside :is() whenever one branch needs a reusable low-specificity rule.

options.text: Independent selector and state release decision case 5: generated content is a reliable place for required instructions.
Independent selector and state release decision case 5: use pseudo-elements for optional presentation, keep required meaning in HTML, and verify the task with generated styles disabled.
Independent selector and state release decision case 5: put required-field instructions only in ::after content because CSS is always loaded.
